us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed Over Iran; Congress Pushes Back on War
us-politics.body:
Two US warplanes were shot down over Iran, with at least one crew member still missing as recovery operations continue. Two US helicopters were also hit during those recovery efforts, raising the human and military cost of the ongoing conflict. The Pentagon has not confirmed full details of the incidents.

On Capitol Hill, Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending Iran operations without a formal declaration of war, and Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about escalation and the prospect of ground troops. GOP lawmakers also rallied around Army Chief of Staff General George after his ouster by Defense Secretary Hegseth. NPR notes Trump's wartime rhetoric diverges sharply from past presidents, a contrast that is drawing scrutiny across the political spectrum.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
GOP praise pours in for Army chief of staff ousted by Hegseth
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran
How Trump's wartime rhetoric differs from past presidents and why that matters

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Draws Fire; Cabinet Shake-Up Looms
us-politics.body:
Trump's budget proposal includes a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction and a significant defense spending plan, but it is already facing Republican resistance. Senator Susan Collins publicly knocked the White House's proposed funding cuts, a rare break from a GOP colleague that signals the budget faces a difficult path in the Senate.

Meanwhile, Trump is weighing further Cabinet changes while trying to avoid what aides describe as a massive shake-up. Todd Blanche has taken over as acting Attorney General at the DOJ, stepping into a role carrying heavy expectations from the White House. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy, though calls for Pam Bondi to testify on the matter continue.

sources.title: Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Mace ‘not backing away’ from Bondi testifying on Epstein

us-politics.headline: States Sue Over Mail-In Voting Order; Colorado Lawyers Revolt
us-politics.body:
A coalition of Democratic-led states filed suit against Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, with Oregon's attorney general calling the order a source of confusion, chaos and distrust. The legal challenges argue the order oversteps federal authority over state election administration. Courts are expected to move quickly given the stakes.

Separately, lawyers in Colorado say the state court's electronic filing system now requires them to certify they will not assist ICE, a requirement critics say puts attorneys in an impossible position. The two disputes reflect a broader battle between states and the federal government over immigration enforcement and election rules, with no resolution in sight.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE
Democratic-led states sue over Trump mail-in ballot order

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order; Senate Confirms Nominees
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting reform in college sports, his second such action aimed at reshaping the NCAA landscape. The order focuses on athlete compensation and eligibility rules, areas where Congress has so far failed to act. Supporters say federal intervention is overdue; critics question whether executive orders are the right tool for sports governance.

In the Senate, Senator Thom Tillis set a clear condition for supporting the attorney general nominee, saying any candidate who does not affirm the legitimacy of January 6 prosecutions will lose his vote. Trump also issued a Good Friday message calling for a resurgence of religion in America. On currency, Senate Democrats pressed the Treasury Department over reports that Trump's name may appear on US banknotes.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
Trump gives rare praise to persistent GOP foe after White House ballroom vote
Senate Democrats press Treasury Department on decision to add Trump name to currency
Tillis sets Jan. 6 red line on attorney general nominee

au-politics.headline: Fuel Shortage Fears Ease But Demand Stays High
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fewer service stations are running dry as Australia's fuel supply stabilises, pushing back against panic-buying that has kept demand elevated. The government is pointing to improved supply chains, but motorists in some regions are still encountering shortages at the bowser.

The Spectator has taken a sharper line, arguing the crisis exposed poor energy policy and urging drivers to take basic precautions like checking tyre pressure rather than waiting on government reassurance. Meanwhile, farmers used the moment to press supermarkets for fairer prices on fresh produce, arguing fuel cost pressures are squeezing margins across the supply chain.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Just, you know, check your tyre pressure
Fuel demand stays high, as farmers urge supermarkets to pay more for fresh produce

au-politics.headline: Nine Charged Over Massive Drug Smuggling Operation
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges after police uncovered an alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia using a so-called mother ship operating in Australian waters. The operation unravelled after a trawler sank, alerting authorities to the syndicate.

Police allege the network was sophisticated and large-scale. The arrests follow a joint investigation involving multiple agencies. No political dimension has emerged yet, but the case will likely renew debate about Australia's maritime border security and the resourcing of federal law enforcement.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Iran Shoots Down US Aircraft, Raising Geopolitical Stakes
au-politics.body:
Iran has downed two US aircraft in an incident analysts say will reverberate sharply in Washington. ABC's coverage frames it as a potential quagmire moment for the Trump administration, drawing comparisons to past US military entanglements in the Middle East.

The incident raises immediate questions about how the US responds and whether it escalates a conflict already stretched across multiple fronts. It comes as Russia continued Good Friday strikes on Ukraine, killing 14 people in daytime attacks on residential areas in Kyiv. Both events point to a volatile international environment with direct implications for Australian foreign and defence policy.

sources.title: Iran's downing of two US aircraft will shock Americans in three ways
Russia launches Good Friday daytime attacks on Ukraine, killing 14

au-politics.headline: Queensland Aged Care CEO Pay Draws Scrutiny
au-politics.body:
Queensland residential aged care executives are earning up to $679,000 a year, according to a new financial review, raising questions about how sector funding is being distributed. The figures sit uncomfortably alongside ongoing concerns about staffing levels and care quality in the sector.

Critics argue high executive pay in a heavily government-subsidised industry warrants closer oversight. Providers counter that competitive salaries are necessary to attract capable leadership. The data lands in an election-sensitive environment where aged care remains a live political issue for both major parties.

sources.title: Qld residential care sector CEOs paid up to $679k

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates Into Direct Exchanges
world.body:
Now in its 36th day, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran has intensified sharply. Tehran downed two American warplanes and launched missile strikes on residential areas in central Israel, while Israel bombed bridges in Lebanon. A US crew member remains missing after a search operation in Iran described as harrowing and dangerous.

The conflict is spreading beyond Iran's borders. Israel struck Lebanese infrastructure, and an intercepted attack on a UAE gas facility at Habshan killed at least one person. The WHO reports more than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint. A French-owned ship passed through the waterway as questions mount over whether military force will be used to keep it open - a move that could dramatically widen the war.

sources.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges
Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
More than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1: WHO
At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack
French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz

world.headline: Easter Observed Amid War From Gaza to Jerusalem
world.body:
Palestinian Christians in Gaza marked Good Friday under blockade and bombardment, while monks held Easter prayers in a largely deserted Old City of Jerusalem. In Lebanon, Christians observed the holy day as Israeli strikes continued nearby.

The scenes underscored how comprehensively the regional conflict has disrupted civilian and religious life across the Middle East. European Parliament member Rima Hassan, a prominent Palestinian-French voice, says she is facing coordinated political harassment for her advocacy.

sources.title: European Parliament’s Rima Hassan: I’m a target of ‘political harassment’
Palestinian Christians in Gaza mark Good Friday
Christians in Lebanon observe Good Friday under Israeli attacks
Monks hold Easter prayers in deserted Old City of Jerusalem

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Bites
world.body:
Cuba has begun releasing more than 2,000 prisoners in what analysts see as a direct response to sustained US pressure. The releases mark one of the largest such moves by Havana in years and signal the government is willing to make concessions to ease its deepening economic isolation.

The timing is notable. Cuba's economy has deteriorated sharply, and the prisoner releases appear calibrated to create diplomatic space with Washington rather than reflect any broader political opening.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Sends Back Stunning Earth Images
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew has released the first photographs of Earth taken from the moon mission, described as spectacular. The images mark a milestone for the program, which aims to return humans to lunar orbit for the first time in over five decades.

The mission is proceeding on schedule. The photos offer a rare human-captured perspective of Earth from deep space and have drawn widespread attention as the program builds toward an eventual crewed lunar landing.

sources.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA releases stunning first photos of Earth from Artemis II moon mission

ai.headline: Anthropic Expands Aggressively While Tightening Its Ecosystem
ai.body:
Anthropic is having a busy week. The company acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee, and is commanding serious attention in private markets - though analysts warn a SpaceX IPO could suck oxygen from the room by competing for the same investor dollars.

On the product side, Anthropic moved to block Claude Code subscribers from using OpenClaw, a third-party client, by requiring them to pay extra. OpenClaw already had security problems drawing scrutiny, giving Anthropic cover for a move that effectively walls off its API from unauthorized wrappers. The decision drew immediate backlash on Hacker News.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Shuffles Executives as Leadership Instability Grows
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo, who joined less than a year ago to run the applications division, is taking medical leave. The company's head of AGI readiness is also stepping back.

The simultaneous departures and reshuffling raise questions about internal stability at a company already navigating a fraught transition from nonprofit to for-profit structure. OpenAI has not detailed timelines for any of the roles.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: Musk Ties SpaceX IPO Access to Buying Grok Subscriptions
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring banks seeking roles in a SpaceX IPO to purchase subscriptions to Grok, his xAI chatbot product. The move blurs the line between business leverage and product promotion in ways that are likely to draw regulatory attention.

Meanwhile, Trump's broader AI ambitions are running into self-inflicted headwinds. His administration's tariffs are raising costs for the data center buildout he has championed, and public opposition to new facilities is stiffening - surveys show people prefer Amazon warehouses to data centers as neighbors.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: AI Erodes Critical Thinking as Meta Suffers Data Breach
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI users increasingly outsource their reasoning to language models, a pattern researchers are calling "cognitive surrender." The findings suggest habitual reliance on LLMs is measurably degrading users' independent logical thinking.

Separately, Meta has paused its work with hiring platform Mercor after a data breach exposed sensitive AI industry information. The breach is significant because Mercor handles recruiting for multiple major AI labs, meaning the compromised data could include proprietary technical and organizational details across the sector.

sources.title: Meta Pauses Work With Mercor After Data Breach Puts AI Industry Secrets at Risk
"Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds

btc.headline: Circle Faces Heat Over $285M Hack and Compliance Failures
btc.body:
Circle is under serious pressure after the $285 million Drift protocol hack exposed its reluctance to freeze stolen USDC. Critics argue the stablecoin issuer had both the technical ability and legal grounds to act but chose not to, raising hard questions about how much control Circle actually exercises over its token.

On-chain investigator ZachXBT piled on, accusing Circle of $420 million in compliance failures dating back to 2022. Together, the two stories paint a damaging picture of a company preparing for an IPO while struggling to defend its role as a responsible financial gatekeeper. The timing could not be worse.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC
ZachXBT accuses Circle of $420M in 'compliance failures' since 2022

btc.headline: Bitcoin ETFs Set to Eclipse Gold as Schwab Joins the Race
btc.body:
Analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, citing Bitcoin's broader and more flexible use cases. Gold ETFs took decades to build their base. Bitcoin is moving faster.

Charles Schwab is accelerating that timeline, announcing plans to launch direct spot Bitcoin and Ether trading in the first half of 2026. That puts one of America's largest retail brokerages squarely in the crypto market, not just offering ETF wrappers but actual spot exposure. For institutional adoption, Schwab's entry carries more weight than another fund launch.

sources.title: Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst
Schwab plans spot bitcoin, ether trading launch in first half of 2026

btc.headline: Quantum Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Not Yet Imminent
btc.body:
Headlines claiming quantum computers can crack Bitcoin in nine minutes are misleading. The research describes a theoretical attack on Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography under highly specific conditions using hardware that does not yet exist at the required scale. Current quantum machines remain far below the threshold needed.

That said, the underlying concern is legitimate. Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations will eventually need upgrading, and the window to plan that transition is finite. The danger is not today's quantum computers. It is complacency about tomorrow's.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Crypto Regulation Tightens From Cambodia to Washington
btc.body:
Cambodian lawmakers are pushing legislation that would impose severe prison sentences on crypto scammers, a sharp response to the country's reputation as a hub for fraud operations. The move signals that even markets outside the Western regulatory orbit are moving toward formal enforcement frameworks.

In the US, community banks are fighting back against the OCC's approval of a trust charter for Coinbase, arguing it gives crypto firms an unfair regulatory shortcut. Meanwhile, the Ethereum Foundation is close to hitting its 70,000 staked ETH target, a milestone that strengthens the network's validator base as staking becomes a mainstream institutional activity.

sources.title: Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers
US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter
Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, buttery cod fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until caramelised and lacquered
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the fish sits, the deeper the umami penetration and the more dramatic the caramelisation under the broiler

exercise.name: The Saturday Deck of Cards
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, some floor space
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight movement to each suit — push-ups, squats, burpees, hollow-body holds — then flip cards one by one and perform the reps shown, turning a lazy Saturday into a genuinely unpredictable full-body session

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a sharp question about whether you are the same person you were ten years ago
learn.why: On a Saturday with some breathing room, tracing this idea from Plutarch through Locke's memory theory to Derek Parfit's radical conclusions about the self is the kind of rabbit hole that quietly rewires how you think about continuity, change, and what you owe your past self
